comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CRITICAL DESIGN CONSTRAINT: Workers run in a V8 isolate, not a full OS process. There is no tokio runtime. The worker crate provides its own async primitives. The LLM adapters must use worker::Fetch for outbound HTTP, not reqwest or hyper. SSE parsing must be manual (line-by-line from the response body stream). The Stream trait from futures may or may not be available — check worker crate compatibility. If not, use a callback/polling approach: read lines from the response body, parse SSE events, yield StreamChunks. The key constraint is that we're streaming FROM the LLM AND simultaneously streaming TO the client via a TransformStream — this is a bidirectional pipe. The Worker stays alive because the response stream is still being written. Test this locally with wrangler dev before assuming it works.

title: Provider trait and StreamChunk enum
description: Create src/providers/mod.rs with: (1) StreamChunk enum { Text(String), Usage(UsageStats), Done }. (2) ProviderError enum covering HTTP errors (with status code + body excerpt), stream disconnect, empty response, timeout (5min no data), and SSE parse errors. (3) A concrete approach for streaming in Workers: since the standard futures::Stream trait may not be available in the wasm32 Workers environment, use a pull-based iterator pattern. The provider returns an LlmStream struct wrapping the Fetch response body reader. LlmStream exposes async fn next_chunk() -> Option<Result<StreamChunk, ProviderError>> which the caller loops over. Internally it delegates to the shared SSE parser, then maps events to StreamChunks via provider-specific logic. (4) Define ProviderParams struct to carry api_key, model, system_prompt, and passthrough params. (5) A resolve_provider(name, env) factory that reads the correct API key secret and returns the right adapter. See PRD section 7.1.
close_reason: Provider trait: StreamChunk enum (Text/Usage/Done), ProviderError enum (6 variants with Display), SseEvent struct, parse_sse_events() parser, error_code_for_provider_error() mapping. 11 unit tests.

(2) Send via worker::Fetch to https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions with Authorization: Bearer OPENAI_API_KEY. (3) Parse SSE stream: for each 'data: {json}' line, extract choices[0].delta.content → emit StreamChunk::Text. On 'data: [DONE]' → emit StreamChunk::Done. On the final event with usage object → emit StreamChunk::Usage with prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, reasoning_tokens (from completion_tokens_details). (4) Handle non-200 responses: read error body, return ProviderError with status and message. See PRD section 7.2.

title: Anthropic adapter — SSE parsing with thinking block filter
description: Create src/providers/anthropic.rs: (1) Build request JSON per PRD section 7.3: model, max_tokens, stream:true, system (if present), thinking config, messages, plus provider_params. (2) Send via worker::Fetch to https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages with x-api-key and anthropic-version:2023-06-01 headers. (3) Parse SSE: maintain current_block_type state. On content_block_start: if type='text' set current to text, if type='thinking' set to thinking. On content_block_delta: if current is text, extract delta.text → emit StreamChunk::Text; if thinking, discard. On content_block_stop: reset current. On message_start: extract input_tokens from usage. On message_delta: extract output_tokens. On message_stop: emit Done. On error event: return ProviderError. Ignore unknown event types. (4) Combine input_tokens (from start) + output_tokens (from delta) into UsageStats. See PRD section 7.3 event table.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: ANTHROPIC THINKING BLOCKS: When extended thinking is enabled, Anthropic returns content blocks of type 'thinking' interleaved with type 'text'. We DISCARD thinking blocks entirely — only text blocks become the round output. This matches APR's behavior (it captures the final response, not the reasoning trace). The thinking content can be very large (10K+ tokens) and isn't useful as a spec revision. If we wanted to capture thinking for debugging, we'd add a separate 'thinking_content' field on the round record — but that's explicitly out of scope for v0.1. Usage tracking: input_tokens come from the message_start event, output_tokens from message_delta. Reasoning/thinking tokens are NOT reported separately by Anthropic (unlike OpenAI's reasoning_tokens field).

title: SSE line parser (shared by both adapters)
description:
Create a shared parse_sse_events() function in src/providers/mod.rs (or a sse.rs sub-module) that takes a ReadableStream body from a Fetch response and yields parsed SSE events. Each event has: event_type (Option<String>, from 'event:' line), data (String, from 'data:' line(s)). SSE spec: events are delimited by blank lines ('

'). Multiple 'data:' lines within one event are concatenated with newlines. Lines starting with ':' are comments (skip). Lines with no colon are ignored. This parser is used by BOTH the OpenAI and Anthropic adapters — they differ only in how they interpret the parsed events, not in how they split the stream into events. Workers constraint: the response body from worker::Fetch is a web_sys::ReadableStream. Use ReadableStreamDefaultReader to read chunks, accumulate into a line buffer, split on '

', yield SSE events. Handle partial chunks (last chunk may not end with '

'). Include detailed console_log traces: log each raw event type + data length for debugging SSE issues in production (gated behind APR_VERBOSE or ENVIRONMENT=development).
close_reason: SSE parser already implemented in providers/mod.rs. Verified working.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: WHY A SHARED SSE PARSER: Both OpenAI and Anthropic use SSE, but with different event structures. Without a shared parser, the SSE line-splitting logic gets duplicated (and bugs get duplicated too). The parser handles the transport layer (splitting bytes into events), while the adapters handle the application layer (interpreting event data as StreamChunks). The parser is also where we add debug logging — every raw SSE event can be logged at VERBOSE level, making production SSE issues debuggable via wrangler tail.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: REBUILD vs INCREMENTAL: This endpoint recomputes everything from scratch. It reads ALL round::workflow::* records (potentially 20+ KV reads), tokenizes each one, and rebuilds the stats chain. This is expensive but necessary for recovery. Use cases: (1) Stats cache corrupted. (2) Rounds manually inserted into KV. (3) Initial import from another system. (4) After deleting and re-running a round. The incremental path (update_stats_after_round in T8_6) is used during normal POST /run flow and only reads 1 key (the existing stats cache). The rebuild path should produce IDENTICAL results to what incremental computation would have produced — this is a testable invariant (PRD success criterion 9).

title: Round Execution

The most complex epic. Reference: PRD sections 5.5 (run route), 7.4-7.5.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: WHY SYNCHRONOUS STREAMING: We chose to stream the LLM response through to the client (keeping the HTTP response open) rather than using waitUntil + 202 polling. Reason: Cloudflare does not guarantee waitUntil keeps an isolate alive for 60 minutes. By keeping the response open, the Worker's lifetime is tied to the LLM stream. Tradeoff: the client must stay connected. If it disconnects, the LLM stream may or may not complete (depends on whether the Worker detects the disconnect). Stuck-run recovery handles this: rounds stuck as 'running' past the lock TTL are reported as 'stale' and can be retried. Fire-and-forget via Durable Objects is deferred to a future version.

title: Sequential round enforcement
description: Add check_previous_round(kv, workflow, round, skip) -> Result<()> to run.rs or storage.rs: if round > 1 and !skip, read round::workflow::(round-1). If it doesn't exist or status != 'complete', return 422 validation_failed: 'Round N-1 must be completed before running round N.' If skip_sequence_check is true in the request body, bypass this check entirely. Round 1 never checks. This prevents convergence data gaps. See PRD section 5.5 (POST /run step 2).

title: Round status state machine

Stale detection: if status='running' and started_at + lock_ttl < now, report as stale. The caller (run handler) uses this to decide whether to proceed, overwrite, or reject. See PRD section 5.5 (POST /run step 3) and GET /rounds stale detection.

title: POST /run — streaming SSE passthrough

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: COMPLEXITY WARNING: This is the most complex task in the project. It wires together every upstream module into a single pipeline: auth → validate workflow → check sequence → check round status → acquire lock → read docs → build prompt → open LLM stream → create TransformStream that simultaneously buffers and forwards → on complete, compute metrics/convergence/save/release. The streaming path creates a ReadableStream that the client consumes. Each SSE event from the LLM is reformatted into our normalized 'event: token' format. The 'event: done' and 'event: error' events are sent AFTER KV writes complete. If KV writes fail after the LLM stream succeeded, we still have the content in the buffer — consider saving it somewhere (even as a failed round with a note that KV post-processing failed). Test this handler more than any other.

Same pipeline as streaming minus the TransformStream — just consume the provider stream to completion, run on-complete, return. Client must wait for full LLM duration. See PRD section 5.5 (non-streaming fallback).

title: On-complete hook — save, compute, update, release

(2) Call update_stats_after_round to incrementally compute convergence. (3) Build the complete Round record with status=complete, content, metrics, convergence, usage, timestamps, duration. (4) Write round record to KV. (5) Update meta record (increment round_count, set latest_round, latest_convergence, updated_at). (6) Release lock. (7) Return summary data for the 'done' SSE event or JSON response. Also add on_round_failed(kv, workflow, round, error, partial_content) for the error path: writes failed round with partial_content, releases lock. See PRD section 7.4 steps a-h.

Also added compute_duration helper.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: ON-COMPLETE HOOK ORDERING: The order of operations matters: (1) Compute metrics first (pure function, can't fail). (2) Update stats cache (KV write, can fail). (3) Write round record (KV write, can fail). (4) Update meta record (KV write, can fail). (5) Release lock (KV delete, can fail). If step 3 fails, the round is lost but the lock will auto-expire. If step 2 fails, stats are stale but can be rebuilt via POST /stats/rebuild. If step 5 fails, the lock auto-expires via expirationTtl. The most important write is step 3 (the round record with content). Consider retrying it once if it fails. Steps 2/4/5 are recoverable.

If missing or status!=complete, return 422: 'Round N-1 must be completed before running round N.' Round 1 always skips. skip_sequence_check:true in request body bypasses. (2) ROUND STATUS MACHINE — check_round_status(kv, wf, round, lock_ttl) -> RoundAction enum: Proceed (no round exists), Retry (failed or stale), Conflict (complete, or running within TTL). Stale detection: status='running' and started_at + lock_ttl < now → stale. Also used by GET /rounds for stale reporting. See PRD section 5.5 POST /run steps 2-3.

title: Storage Layer
description: KV abstraction: typed read/write/list/delete helpers, key pattern construction, and lock management. Every route that touches state depends on this layer. Reference: PRD section 6.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: KV CONSISTENCY MODEL: Cloudflare KV is eventually consistent with a ~60s propagation delay for writes. In practice, for our single-tenant use case, the Worker that wrote a value can read it back immediately (same datacenter). But if two Workers in different datacenters write the same key, last-writer-wins with no merge. This is why the lock is advisory — two simultaneous requests from different edge locations could both acquire it. The lock uses KV's built-in expirationTtl for auto-cleanup, which is the most reliable way to handle stale locks without a background sweeper.

All operations take a worker::kv::KvStore binding. Error handling: KV failures map to InternalError via the error module. The list function returns (keys, cursor) for pagination support. See PRD section 6 for the KV namespace binding name 'APRP'.

The Lock struct has round, started_at, expires_at. The default TTL comes from the DEFAULT_LOCK_TTL_SECONDS env var. comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: TEST STRATEGY: Unit tests run without KV or network — they test pure functions (convergence math, template parsing, validation regex, metrics). Integration tests need mock KV and mock HTTP endpoints. For Workers, this means either: (1) Using wrangler dev with miniflare (local emulation), (2) Building a mock KV wrapper in tests, or (3) Using cargo test with the worker crate's test utilities. The worker crate has limited test support — option (2) is likely most practical. For SSE parsing tests, create mock SSE streams as string data and feed them through the parser. For E2E, use real API keys against real LLM endpoints — these are expensive and should only run manually.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: TESTING PHILOSOPHY: Tests are split per-module so they can be written in parallel by different agents, tracked independently, and run in CI as separate test targets. Every test must log: (1) Test name. (2) Input summary (what was sent). (3) Expected output. (4) Actual output. (5) PASS/FAIL verdict. This logging is NOT optional — it is the primary debugging tool when tests fail in CI or on a remote Worker. Unit tests use #[test] and run via cargo test with no network or KV. Integration tests use a mock KV and mock HTTP. E2E tests hit a real deployed Worker with real LLM APIs — they are expensive and run manually, but their scripts must be checked in and runnable by anyone with the right env vars.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: VALIDATION ORDERING: Template validation and document-exists validation happen at workflow save time, not just at run time. This catches misconfigurations early. The validation order is: (1) Name/provider/model format checks. (2) If template provided, extract placeholders and verify each exists in documents map. (3) If template_with_impl provided, same check. (4) For each role in documents map, verify the KV key doc::workflow::role exists. (5) Warn on unreferenced roles. Steps 1-3 are fast (no KV reads). Step 4 requires N KV reads where N = number of document roles (typically 2-3). Step 5 is in-memory set comparison. This means: documents MUST be uploaded BEFORE creating the workflow (or the doc-exists check fails). The API flow is: PUT docs → POST workflow → POST run.

(4) Delete meta::workflow, stats::workflow, lock::workflow. (5) Count successful deletes. (6) Return {deleted: name, keys_removed: N}. Best-effort: if some deletes fail after config is removed, orphaned keys are harmless. See PRD section 5.5 (DELETE /workflows/:name).
